elements.sent-2411-001: The Newman-Unti-Tamburino (NUT) solution is characterized as the unique Petrov Type $D$ vacuum metric such that the two double principal null directions form an integrable distribution.
elements.sent-2411-002: The uniqueness of the NUT is established by evaluating the integrability conditions of the Newman-Penrose equations up to $SL(2,C)$ transformations, resulting in a coordinate-free characterization of the solution.
elements.sent-2411-003: The solution strategy for obtaining exact solutions of Einstein's equations is to start with a metric ansatz in a given coordinate frame, possibly with certain symmetry conditions and pre-determined sources together with their field equations.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this scheme, Einstein's equations are second-order partial differential equations for the metric functions.
elements.sent-2411-005: The Newman-Penrose (NP) formalism \cite{Newman:1961qr proposed in the early '60s, proved to be a useful tool for obtaining exact solutions in particular for the study of the asymptotic behavior of gravitational fields \cite{Stephani:2003tm .}}
elements.sent-2411-006: The Newman-Penrose formalism is actually a special case of the moving frame approach, in which the connection is defined either in terms of the commutators of frame as sections of the tangent bundle, of exterior derivatives of the frame as sections of the cotangent bundle.
elements.sent-2411-007: Then, the curvature is defined by Cartan's structural equations and the Bianchi identities give relations among the derivatives of the curvature components.
elements.sent-2411-008: In the NP formalism, the moving frame consists of $4$ null vector fields, called the ``null tetrad".
elements.sent-2411-009: The NP equations are first-order partial differential equations for the components of the connection and of the curvature where directional derivative operators are dual to the $1$-forms of the moving frame.
elements.sent-2411-010: The components of the connection are called ``spin coefficients".
elements.sent-2411-011: The Newman-Penrose equations are explicit expressions for the directional derivatives of the spin coefficients and they can be considered as defining the curvature.
elements.sent-2411-012: The components of the Weyl and Ricci tensors appear explicitly in the NP equations and explicit expressions of the Bianchi identities giving directional derivatives of the components of the curvature are part of the NP system of equations.
elements.sent-2411-013: Explicit expressions of the transformations of the NP quantities under the transformations of the null tetrad, called as the ``gauge transformations" are given in \cite{Carmeli:1975wq .}
elements.sent-2411-014: The commutation relations of the null tetrad, the NP equations, the Bianchi identities and the gauge transformations constitute a coordinate-free set-up for Einstein's equations.
elements.sent-2411-015: In the literature, the null tetrad is usually defined in terms of the metric, and coordinate transformations are used to express the final form of the exact solution in terms of local coordinates.
elements.sent-2411-016: In the present work we are attempting an approach to the characterization of exact solutions in a coordinate-free manner via the integrability conditions of the NP equations.
elements.sent-2411-017: Namely, we propose to start with certain algebraic and geometrical ansatze expressed in terms of the NP quantities and study the integrability of the resulting system of equations.
elements.sent-2411-018: Under geometric and algebraic assumptions, the NP system will usually be overdetermined, and a systematic study of the integrability conditions in the framework of the Riquier-Janet theory \cite{schwarz1984 will result in either an inconsistent system or a system in ``involution" for which the existence of a local solution is guaranteed.}
elements.sent-2411-019: The freedom in a system in involution is expressed in terms of functions that are not determined by the integrability conditions.
elements.sent-2411-020: We can say that the system in involution defines an exact solution, if the arbitrariness in the system can be eliminated by a coordinate transformation.
elements.sent-2411-021: Although solving the NP equations by the method described above is a coordinate-free approach, without referring explicitly to the metric, it may be necessary to ``reconstruct" the metric in order to deal with the elimination of the coordinate freedom.
elements.sent-2411-022: In the present work, we are applying this method to re-derive the well-known Newman-Unti-Tamburino (NUT) \cite{Newman:1963yy solution, as a special type of Type $D$ vacuum metrics.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Here, we are assuming that the metric is vacuum and Petrov Type D; the geometric ansatz is the assumption that the repeated null directions of the Weyl tensor form an integrable distribution.
elements.sent-2411-024: In terms of NP quantities this assumption leads to the condition $\tau+\bar{\pi =0$, and corresponds to the group (Type I) of Type D vacuum metrics as classified by \cite{Kinnersley:1969zza .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Type D metrics with repeated null directions forming an integrable distribution will be denoted as ``NUT background".
elements.sent-2411-026: Non-vacuum solutions on NUT backgrounds are currently investigated by the present authors, via the computation of integrability conditions.
elements.sent-2411-027: We recall that the Petrov classification \cite{Stephani:2003tm is based on the structure of the eigenvectors of the Weyl tensor.}
elements.sent-2411-028: In this classification, Type $D$ metrics are characterized by the existence of $2$ principal null directions hence the spacetime has a natural $2+2$ decomposition.
elements.sent-2411-029: The Schwarzchild, NUT, Reissner-Nördstrom, and Kerr metrics are among physically important Type $D$ vacuum solutions \cite{ahsan .}
elements.sent-2411-030: As mentioned above, the Newman-Penrose formalism, introduced in \cite{Newman:1961qr provides a natural setting for studying metrics in terms of null congruences.}
elements.sent-2411-031: In fact, many of the solutions mentioned above have been obtained in the framework of the NP formalism.
elements.sent-2411-032: We present the necessary mathematical formulations in Section \ref{sec2 .}
elements.sent-2411-033: Section \ref{sec3 is devoted to the integrability of the NP equations for the NUT solution in the case $\rho-\bar\rho\ne 0$.}
elements.sent-2411-034: The case $\rho=\bar\rho$ is discussed in Section \ref{sec4 .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Finally, in Section \ref{sec5 , we study the reconstruction of the metric, and we prove that the remaining freedom in the solution of the involutive system corresponds to a diffeomorphism of the underlying manifold.}
elements.sent-2411-036: We give a list of the NP equations for general Type $D$ metrics and $SL(2,C)$ transformations for the NUT case in appendices.
elements.sent-2411-037: The Ricci tensor $R_{ij}$ and the Ricci scalar $R$, are obtained via contractions (using Einstein's summation convention for repeated indices), as $R_{ij =R^k_{ikj}$ and $R=R^i_i$.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Einstein's equations are then \begin{equation}R_{ij}-\frac {1}{2}Rg_{ij}+\Lambda g_{ij}=\kappa T_{ij},\end{equation} where $\Lambda $ is the cosmological constant, $\kappa$ is the gravitational constant and $T_{ij}$ is the energy momentum tensor.
elements.sent-2411-039: If the sources are specified and no additional condition is imposed, these equations form a system of second-order partial differential equations with $10$ equations for $10$ unknowns.
elements.sent-2411-040: In particular, the number of equations should be equal to the number of unknowns.
elements.sent-2411-041: The well-known Cauchy-Kovalevskaya (CK) theorem states that if a system of partial differential equations is in normal form then there exists a unique solution in a neighborhood of the initial (non-characteristic) surface.
elements.sent-2411-042: For some physical applications of the CK theorem, one can see \cite{maia,izumi2013analysis,ita2008proposal,klainerman1999local .}
elements.sent-2411-043: Systems where the number of equations is greater than the number of variables are called over-determined.
elements.sent-2411-044: An analogue of the CK theorem for the existence of solutions is the Riquier-Janet theorem for over-determined systems \cite{schwarz1984 .}
elements.sent-2411-045: This theorem states that if the system is in ``involution", then there is an analytic solution where the free parameters are determined from the structure of the system in involution.
elements.sent-2411-046: This method of solution is based on the theory of over-determined systems studied at the turn of the 20th century by Riquier and Janet.
elements.sent-2411-047: This method is based on the computation of integrability conditions and results in either an inconsistent system with no solutions or in a system in involution, the existence of a local analytic solution being guaranteed by the Riquier-Janet existence theorem, an analogue of the CK theorem for overdetermined systems.
elements.sent-2411-048: For example, the Riemann-Lanczos problem can be studied using the Riquier-Janet theorem \cite{lanczos1949lagrangian, bampi1983third,dolan2003exterior .}
elements.sent-2411-049: As an example of the the effect of the integrability conditions we present a system discussed in reference \cite{brans1967computer where it is shown that only the trivial solution exists.}
elements.sent-2411-050: In this system, the independent variables and the dependent variables are respectively $\{x,y,z\}$, and $\{u,v,w\}$.
elements.sent-2411-051: If the system of equations were of the form \begin{equation}u_x=-vx,\quad v_x=uy, \quad w_z=u+\epsilon,\end{equation} then the first two equations would be a normal system to which CK theorem could be applied and $w$ would then be solved.
elements.sent-2411-052: But if we add a third equation for $u$ and $v$ as below \begin{equation}u_x=-vx,\quad u_y=-wz,\quad v_x=uy, \quad w_z=u+\epsilon.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-053: Then the $y$ derivative of the first equation should be consistent with the $x$ derivative of the second equation, since mixed partial derivatives commute.
elements.sent-2411-054: The addition of this new equation leads to an overdetermined system and in \cite{brans1967computer it is shown that there is no nontrivial solution.}
elements.sent-2411-055: The key ingredient of the study of integrability conditions is using the fact that mixed partial derivatives commute.
elements.sent-2411-056: The tricky part is to define a total order on the set of the partial derivatives of the dependent variables and solve each equation with respect to the derivatives with the highest rank with respect to this order \cite{schwarz1984 .}
elements.sent-2411-057: An extensive bibliography on this formalism can be found in the reference \cite{Stephani2003 .}
elements.sent-2411-058: The Newman-Penrose equations are first-order equations defined by directional derivatives.
elements.sent-2411-059: The method ordering of partial derivatives and computing integrability conditions can be applied to the Newman-Penrose system, provided that we use the commutation relations appropriately.
elements.sent-2411-060: In the Newman-Penrose system, the ordering of derivatives are chosen as follows.
elements.sent-2411-061: In the spirit of first-order formulation, we work in a scheme where we use the Bianchi identities as differential equations for the Weyl and Ricci spinors.
elements.sent-2411-062: Thus, the unknowns are $\Psi_i$, ($i=0,\dots,4$), $\Phi_{ij}$, ($i,j=0,1,2$) and $\Lambda $ and the ranks of the $\Psi_i$'s, $\Phi_{ij}$'s and $\Lambda$ are chosen the be higher compared to the spin coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-063: As for the derivatives, motivated by the decomposition of the NP equations into radial and non-radial equations, commonly used in the study of exact solutions, we rank the directional derivatives as \begin{equation}D>\Delta>\delta>\overline{\delta}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-064: Following the general conventions adopted in the literature, if the null vector $l$ is the repeated principal null direction, then $\Psi_0=\Psi_1=0$, and Type $D$ metrics are characterized by the equality $3\Psi_2\Psi_4=2\Psi_3^2$, and by using $SL(2,C)$ rotations it is possible to set $\Psi_3=\Psi_4=0$.
elements.sent-2411-065: In addition, for the vacuum case, $\Psi_2$ turns out to be the only surviving curvature component.
elements.sent-2411-066: In \cite{Kinnersley:1969zza , Kinnersley obtained all Type $D$ vacuum metrics using the Newman-Penrose formalism.}
elements.sent-2411-067: Several cases yield ten solutions which can coarsely be divided into $\rho \neq 0$ and $\rho=0$ subcases.
elements.sent-2411-068: The NUT metric \cite{Newman:1963yy corresponds to the case $\rho \neq 0, \pi_0=\tau_0=0$.}
elements.sent-2411-069: The null tetrad can be chosen as \begin{eqnarray}l^{\mu} = \delta^{2}_{\mu}, \nonumber n^{\mu} = U \delta_{2}^{\mu} + \delta_1^{\mu}, \nonumber m^{\mu} = \overline{\rho} (\xi^{\circ^1}\delta_1^{\mu} +\xi^{\circ^3}\delta_3^{\mu} +\xi^{\circ^4}\delta_4^{\mu}),\end{eqnarray} where \begin{equation}\xi^{\circ^1}=(\bar{\rho}p R^3)^{-1}(A^{\circ}+iB^{\circ}),\quad \xi^{\circ^3}=-(\bar{\rho}p R^3)^{-1},\quad \xi^{\circ^4}=-i(\bar{\rho}p R^3)^{-1}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-070: Thus the covariant form of the null tetrad is \begin{eqnarray}l_{\mu} = \delta_{\mu}^1 - A^{\circ} \delta_{\mu}^3-B^{\circ} \delta_{\mu}^4 , \nonumber n_{\mu} = \delta_{\mu}^2+U\left[-\delta_{\mu}^1 + A^{\circ} \delta_{\mu}^3+B^{\circ} \delta_{\mu}^4\right], \nonumber m_{\mu} = - \left(\delta_{\mu}^3 + i \delta_{\mu}^4\right)/2 \rho p.\end{eqnarray}.
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}l_{\mu} = \delta_{\mu}^1 - A^{\circ} \delta_{\mu}^3-B^{\circ} \delta_{\mu}^4 , \nonumber n_{\mu} = \delta_{\mu}^2+U\left[-\delta_{\mu}^1 + A^{\circ} \delta_{\mu}^3+B^{\circ} \delta_{\mu}^4\right], \nonumber m_{\mu} = - \left(\delta_{\mu}^3 + i \delta_{\mu}^4\right)/2 \rho p.\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-009: \begin{equation}dl = -(\epsilon+\bar{\epsilon}) ln +(\alpha+\bar{\beta}-\bar{\tau}) lm +(\bar{\alpha}+{\beta}-{\tau}) l\bar{m} -\bar{\kappa} nm -\kappa n\bar{m} +(\rho-\bar{\rho}) m\bar{m}, dn = -(\gamma+\bar{\gamma}) ln +\nu lm +\bar{\nu} l\bar{m} +(\pi-\alpha-\bar{\beta}) nm +(\bar{\pi}-\bar{\alpha}-{\beta}) n\bar{m} +(\mu-\bar{\mu}) m\bar{m}, dm = -(\tau+\bar{\pi}) ln +(\bar{\mu}+\gamma-\bar{\gamma}) lm +\bar{\lambda} l\bar{m} +(\epsilon-\bar{\epsilon}-\rho) nm -\sigma n\bar{m} -(\bar{\alpha}-\beta) m\bar{m}.\end{equation}
elements.section-2411-003: Coordinate-Free Approach to the NUT solution
elements.sent-2411-072: In the present work, we re-derive the NUT solution, using integrability conditions, as a coordinate-free derivation.
elements.sent-2411-073: The NUT solution is a vacuum metric that belongs to the class of Type $D$ metrics in the Petrov classification \cite{Newman:1963yy .}
elements.sent-2411-074: In Newman-Penrose formalism, a vacuum solution is characterized by \begin{equation}\Phi_{ij}=\Lambda=0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-075: For Type $D$ metrics, the tetrad can be chosen in such a way that only $\Psi_2$ is nonzero, that is $\Psi_0=\Psi_1=\Psi_3=\Psi_4=0.$ Under these conditions, the Bianchi identities that involve $\Psi_2$ algebraically reduce to the following set of equations.. \begin{equation}3 \kappa\Psi_2=0,\quad 3 \sigma \Psi_2=0, \quad 3 \lambda \Psi_2=0, \quad 3 \nu \Psi_2=0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-076: Thus, as $\Psi_2$ is nonzero, for all Type $D$ vacuum metrics with repeated principal null direction $l^\mu$, we have \begin{equation}\kappa=\sigma=\nu=\lambda=0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-077: The remaining Bianchi identities for Type $D$ metrics are \begin{equation}D\Psi_2=3 \rho \Psi_2,\quad\quad \Delta\Psi_2=-3\mu \Psi_2,\quad\quad \bar{\delta}\Psi_2=-3\pi\Psi_2,\quad\quad \delta\Psi_2=3 \tau\Psi_2.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-078: As stated in \cite{{Kinnersley:1969zza , the NUT solution is characterized by the additional constraints \begin{equation}\tau=\pi=0.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-079: We will show that this condition can be expressed geometrically and the NUT solution turns out to be a special case of Type $D$ vacuum metrics, where the spin coefficients satisfy the following relations \begin{equation}\kappa=\nu=\sigma=\lambda=\epsilon=\tau=\pi= \overline{\alpha}+\beta=0, \quad \gamma-\bar\gamma= \textstyle{\frac{1}{2}}\left( {\frac{\Psi_2}{\rho}-\frac{\bar{\Psi}_2}{\bar\rho}}\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-080: The conjugate of Eq.(\ref{8c ) is \begin{equation}\Delta \overline{\alpha}=\delta{\overline{\gamma}}+\overline{\alpha}(\gamma-\overline{\gamma}-\mu),\end{equation} and substituing this in Eq.(\ref{8d ) we get \begin{eqnarray}\delta(\gamma+\overline{\gamma}) = 0, \overline{\delta}(\gamma+\overline{\gamma}) = 0.\end{eqnarray}.}}
elements.sent-2411-081: After adding these algebraic relations, the systems for $\alpha$ and $\gamma$ are now, \begin{eqnarray}D\alpha = \rho\alpha, \label{9a} \delta \alpha +\overline{\delta}\overline{\alpha} = \textstyle{\frac{1}{2}}(\rho\mu+\bar{\rho}\bar{\mu}) +4\alpha\overline{\alpha} +\textstyle{\frac{1}{2}}(\gamma-\bar{\gamma})(\rho-\bar{\rho}) -\textstyle{\frac{1}{2}}(\Psi_2+\overline{\Psi}_2), \label{9b} \Delta\alpha-\overline\delta{\gamma} = \alpha(\overline{\gamma}-\gamma-\overline{\mu}), \label{9c}\end{eqnarray} The left-hand side of the equation (\ref{9b ) is real, thus the right-hand side has to be zero, namely, \begin{equation}(\gamma+\overline\gamma)(\rho-\overline\rho)+\mu\rho-\bar{\mu}\bar{\rho}-(\Psi_2-\overline\Psi_2)=0. \label{10}\end{equation} It follows that $(\gamma+\overline\gamma)$ is determined provided that $(\rho-\overline\rho)$ is nonzero.}
elements.sent-2411-082: The case $\rho=\overline{\rho}$ will be studied in section \ref{sec4 .}
elements.sent-2411-084: System of equations for $\Psi_2$: The commutator of $D$ and $\Delta$ applied to $\Psi_2$ gives \begin{equation}\Delta D \Psi_2 - D \Delta \Psi_2= \left(\gamma+\overline{\gamma}\right)D\Psi_2.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-085: Substituting the expressions of $D\Psi_2$ and $\Delta\Psi_2$, we obtain, \begin{equation}3 \Psi_2 \left(\Delta \rho \right)+3 \rho \left(\Delta \Psi_2 \right)+3 \Psi_2 \left(D \mu\right)+ 3 \mu \left(D \Psi_2\right)=\left(\gamma+ \overline{\gamma}\right)D \Psi_2.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-086: Finally, after substituting $D\mu$, $\Delta\rho$, $D\Psi$ and $\Delta\Psi$, we obtain the algebraic relation \begin{equation}\mu \overline{\rho}-\overline{\mu} \rho=0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-087: Then, the commutator of $D$ and $\overline{\delta}$, \begin{equation}\overline{\delta} D\Psi_2 - D \overline{\delta} \Psi_2=-\rho\overline{\delta}\Psi_2,\end{equation} results in \begin{equation}3 \Psi_2 \left(\overline{\delta}\rho\right) + 3 \rho \left(\overline{\delta} \Psi_2\right)=0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-089: Similarly, the commutator of $\Delta$ and $\delta$ gives \begin{equation}\delta\mu=0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-090: As a final step, applying the commutator of $\delta$ and $\bar{\delta}$ we again get the condition $\mu\bar{\rho =\bar{\mu}\rho$.}
elements.sent-2411-091: At this stage, all derivatives of $\Psi_2$ are consistently defined and the only freedom in $\Psi_2$ is an arbitrary constant.
elements.sent-2411-092: Actually, we can ``solve" $\Psi_2$ as \begin{equation}\Psi_2=\rho^3\Psi_{20},\end{equation} and it can be checked that \begin{equation}D\Psi_{20}=\Delta\Psi_{20}=\delta\Psi_{20}=\bar{\delta}\Psi_{20}=0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-093: It follows that $\Psi_{20}$ is a complex constant.
elements.sent-2411-094: System of equations for $\rho$: All derivatives of $\rho$ are now determined and the application of the commutation relation of $\delta$ and $\bar\delta$ to $\rho$ gives \begin{equation}(\bar\mu-\mu)D\rho+(\bar\rho-\rho)\Delta\rho=0,\end{equation} the following algebraic relation \begin{equation}2\mu\rho\overline\rho-2\mu\overline{\rho}^2-\Psi_2\overline\rho +\overline\Psi_2\rho=0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-095: Using the relation $\mu\bar{\rho =\bar{\mu}\rho$, we can write this in a more symmetrical form \begin{equation}2\rho\overline\rho(\mu-\overline\mu)-\Psi_2\overline\rho +\overline\Psi_2\rho=0.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-096: Hence the imaginary part of $\mu$ is determined as \begin{equation}\mu-\bar{\mu}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\Psi_2}{\rho}-\frac{\bar{\Psi}_2}{\bar{\rho}}\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-097: At this stage, the only freedom in $\rho$ is also an arbitrary constant as in the case of $\Psi_2$.
elements.sent-2411-098: It can be seen that \begin{equation}D\left(\frac{1}{\rho}+\frac{1}{\bar{\rho}}\right)=-2,\quad D\left(\frac{1}{\rho}-\frac{1}{\bar{\rho}}\right)=0.\quad\end{equation} Thus, up to a coordinate transformation, $\rho$ has the well-known form \begin{equation}\rho=-(r+\rho^\circ)^{-1},\end{equation} where $\rho^\circ$ is a pure imaginary constant.
elements.sent-2411-099: Finally, replacing $\bar{\mu =\mu(\bar{\rho /\rho)$, in the expression for the imaginary part of $\mu$, we obtain the definition of $\mu$ as \begin{equation}\mu=\frac{1}{2}\left[1-\frac{\bar{\rho}}{\rho}\right]^{-1} \left(\frac{\Psi_2}{\rho}-\frac{\bar{\Psi}_2}{\bar{\rho}}\right).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-100: It has been checked that the differential equations for $\mu$ are satisfied without giving any new integrability constraint.
elements.sent-2411-101: System of equations and gauge transformation for $\gamma$: In Appendix \ref{appb , we show that gauge transformation can be used to fix the imaginary part of $\gamma$.}
elements.sent-2411-102: Together with the algebraic relation for its real part, we obtain \begin{equation}\gamma=\frac{1}{2}\frac{\Psi_2}{\rho},\end{equation} and all derivatives are again identically satisfied.
elements.sent-2411-103: System of equations for $\alpha$ and the freedom in the metric: The equation $D\alpha=\rho\alpha$ can be solved as \begin{equation}\alpha=\rho \alpha_0,\quad D\alpha_0=0.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-107: After substitutions, the equation for $\alpha$ is now \begin{equation}\delta\alpha+\overline\delta\overline\alpha -4\alpha\overline\alpha=-\frac{1}{2}\frac{1}{\rho-\overline{\rho}} \frac{1}{\rho\bar{\rho}} \left[\bar{\Psi}_2\rho^3-\Psi_2\bar{\rho}^3\right].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-108: This is a non-radial equation for $\alpha_0$.
elements.sent-2411-109: In Section \ref{sec5 , we will reconstruct the metric and show that this equation implies that the $2$-dimensional space-like submanifold with tangent fields $m$ and $\overline{m}$ has constant curvature.}

elements.sent-2411-115: It can be checked that the commutation relations of the derivatives of $\Psi_2$ are identically satisfied and the same holds for the derivatives of $\rho$ and $\mu$.

elements.sent-2411-118: In the application of NP formalism, one starts with a metric in a given coordinate system and writes down the directional derivative operators as linear combinations of the partial derivative operators.
elements.sent-2411-119: In the coordinate-free approach, we expand the directional derivative operators $D$, $\Delta$, and $\delta$ in terms of a commuting set of derivative operators, and the coefficients of this expansion turn out to be the metric functions.
elements.sent-2411-120: Let $\Delta_0$ and $\delta_0$ be differential operators commuting with $D$.
elements.sent-2411-122: In fact, the commutation relation of $D$ and $\Delta$ gives \begin{eqnarray}(UD+\Delta_0)D-D(UD+\Delta_0) = (\gamma+\bar\gamma)D, \nonumber U D^2+\Delta_0 D-(DU)D-D\Delta_0-U D^2 = (\gamma+\bar\gamma)D, \nonumber (\Delta_0 D- D\Delta_0)-(DU)D = (\gamma+\bar\gamma)D,\end{eqnarray} as $D$ and $\Delta_0$ commute, we get \begin{equation}DU=-(\gamma+\bar\gamma).\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-134: Using $\delta_0\rho=0$ and $\bar\delta_0\bar\rho=0$, and dividing the commutation relation by $\rho\bar\rho$, we obtain \begin{eqnarray}\bar\delta_0\delta_0- \delta_0\bar\delta_0 = (\rho\bar\rho)^{-1} \left[ -\textstyle\frac{1}{2}\bar\rho\frac{\Psi_2}{\rho^2} +\textstyle\frac{1}{2}\rho\frac{\bar\Psi_2}{\bar\rho^2} +(\bar\rho-\rho)U_0 \right]D +(\rho\bar\rho)^{-1} (\bar\rho-\rho)\Delta_0 +2\alpha_0 \delta_0 -2\bar\alpha_0 \delta_0,\nonumber = \left[ -\textstyle\frac{1}{2}\Psi_{20} +\textstyle\frac{1}{2}\bar\Psi_{20} +(\rho\bar\rho)^{-1}(\bar\rho-\rho)U_0 \right]D +(\rho\bar\rho)^{-1} (\bar\rho-\rho)\Delta_0 +2\alpha_0 \delta_0 -2\bar\alpha_0 \delta_0.\nonumber\end{eqnarray} Recall that \begin{equation}\label{55} \frac{\bar\rho-\rho}{\rho\bar\rho}=\frac{1}{\rho}-\frac{1}{\bar\rho}=-2\rho_0,\end{equation} hence \begin{equation}\bar\delta_0\delta_0- \delta_0\bar\delta_0= \left[ -\textstyle\frac{1}{2}\Psi_{20} +\textstyle\frac{1}{2}\bar\Psi_{20} -2\rho_0 U_0 \right]D -2\rho_0 \Delta_0 +2\alpha_0 \delta_0 -2\bar\alpha_0 \delta_0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-135: As the coefficient of $D$ is zero, we have \begin{equation}-\textstyle\frac{1}{2}\Psi_{20} +\textstyle\frac{1}{2}\bar\Psi_{20} -2\rho_0 U_0=0.\end{equation}. Thus \begin{equation}\bar\delta_0\delta_0- \delta_0\bar\delta_0= -2\rho_0 \Delta_0 +2\alpha_0 \delta_0 -2\bar\alpha_0 \delta_0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-136: We know that if a manifold $M$ has topological splitting $M=M_1\times M_2$, then the connection on $M$ induces a connection on each submanifold.
elements.sent-2411-137: In the present case, the connection on the $2$-dimensional submanifold with the tangent frame $m$ and $\overline{m}$ is defined by restricting $dm$ to this submanifold.
elements.sent-2411-138: We also recall that if $\{U_1,U_2\}$ is an orthonormal frame on a $2$ dimensional submanifold, then the connection is defined by a $1$-form $\omega$ satisfying the equations \begin{equation}dU_1=\omega U_2,\quad dU_2=-\omega U_1,\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\omega=\omega_1 U_1+\omega_2 U_2.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-139: Then the curvature of the submanifold is defined by the $2-$form $\Omega$ \begin{equation}\Omega=d\omega-\omega\wedge \omega.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-140: In our case, $dm$ restricted to the submanifold gives \begin{equation}dm_{|F}=(\beta-\overline{\alpha})m\overline{m}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-141: In order to compute the induced connection, we separate the real and imaginary parts as \begin{equation}m=m_1+im_2,\quad \alpha=\alpha_1+i\alpha_2.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-142: Separating the real and imaginary parts, we obtain \begin{equation}dm_1=4\alpha_2 m_1 m_2,\quad dm_2=4 \alpha_1 m_1 m_2,\end{equation} thus \begin{equation}\omega=4 \alpha_2 m_1 +4\alpha_1 m_2.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-143:
We also note that as the product $\omega \wedge \omega=0$, $\Omega=d\omega$.  egin{eqnarray}\Omega = 4 \left[ d\alpha_2 m_1 + d\alpha_1 m_2 +\alpha_2 dm_1+\alpha_1 dm_2 ight], 
onumber \Omega = 4 \left[ d\alpha_2 m_1 + d\alpha_1 m_2 +\alpha_2 4 \alpha_2 m_1 m_2 +\alpha_1 4 \alpha_1 m_1 m_2 ight], 
onumber \Omega = 4 \left[ d\alpha_2 m_1 + d\alpha_1 m_2 + 4(\alpha_1^2 +\alpha_2^2) m_1 m_2 ight]. 
onumber\end{eqnarray} Now we convert to the complex notation;  egin{equation}\alpha_1^2 +\alpha_2^2=\alpha\overline{\alpha},\quad m_1m_2= rac{i}{2}m\overline{m},\end{equation} and use the decomposition of the exterior derivative on $M$ as  egin{equation}d=\Delta l + D n -\overline{\delta} m-\delta \overline{m},\end{equation} to get  egin{equation}\Omega=-2 \left[ \delta\alpha+\overline\delta\overline\alpha -4\alpha\overline\alphaight]m\overline{m}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-073:
egin{equation}\Omega = 4 \left[ d\alpha_2 m_1 + d\alpha_1 m_2 +\alpha_2 dm_1+\alpha_1 dm_2 ight], 
onumber \Omega = 4 \left[ d\alpha_2 m_1 + d\alpha_1 m_2 +\alpha_2 4 \alpha_2 m_1 m_2 +\alpha_1 4 \alpha_1 m_1 m_2 ight], 
onumber \Omega = 4 \left[ d\alpha_2 m_1 + d\alpha_1 m_2 + 4(\alpha_1^2 +\alpha_2^2) m_1 m_2 ight]. 
onumber\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-144: Eliminating the $\rho$ dependency and using the equation \begin{equation}\delta_0\alpha_0+\overline\delta_0\overline\alpha_0 -4\alpha_0\overline\alpha_0=-\frac{1}{4\rho_0} \left[\bar{\Psi}_{20}-\Psi_{20}\right],\end{equation} we see that the submanifold $F$ has constant curvature.
elements.section-2411-006: NP equations for general Type $D$ metrics
elements.sent-2411-145: The set of NP equations for general Type $D$ metrics can be given as \begin{eqnarray}D\rho = \rho^2, D\tau = (\tau+\bar\pi)\rho, D\alpha = \rho\alpha+\rho\pi, D\beta = \bar\rho\beta, D\gamma = (\tau+\bar\pi)\alpha+ (\bar\tau+\pi)\beta+\tau\pi+\Psi_2, -\bar\delta\pi = \pi^2+(\alpha-\bar\beta)\pi, D\mu-\delta\pi = \bar\rho\mu+\pi\bar\pi -\pi(\bar\alpha-\beta)+\Psi_2, -\Delta \pi = (\pi+\bar\tau)\mu+(\gamma-\bar\gamma)\mu, \delta\rho = \rho(\bar\alpha+\beta) +(\rho-\bar\rho)\tau, \delta\alpha-\bar\delta\beta = \mu\rho+\alpha\bar\alpha +\beta\bar\beta-2\alpha\beta +\gamma(\rho-\bar\rho)-\Psi_2, -\bar\delta\mu = (\mu-\bar\mu)\pi+\mu(\alpha+\bar\beta), -\Delta\mu = \mu^2 +(\gamma+\bar\gamma)\mu, \delta\gamma-\Delta\beta = (\tau-\bar\alpha-\beta)\gamma+\mu\tau -\beta(\gamma-\bar\gamma-\mu), \delta\tau = (\tau+\beta-\bar\alpha)\tau, \Delta\rho-\bar\delta\tau = -\rho\bar\mu +(\bar\beta -\alpha -\bar\tau) \tau + (\gamma+\bar \gamma)\rho -\Psi_2, \Delta\alpha-\bar\delta\gamma = (\bar\gamma-\bar\mu)\alpha+(\bar\beta-\bar\tau)\gamma.\end{eqnarray}.
elements.sent-2411-146: Explicit expressions of the transformations of the spin coefficients and the curvature components are given in \cite{Carmeli:1975wq .}
elements.sent-2411-147: These transformations are arranged in three groups:.
elements.sent-2411-148: Type $D$ metrics with repeated principal null direction are characterized by $\kappa=\nu=0$, therefore the first and the third groups of null rotations are not allowed.
elements.sent-2411-149: The conditions $\kappa=\nu=\lambda=\sigma=0$ are preserved under these transformations and $\Psi_2$ is invariant.
elements.sent-2411-150: We can list the transformations of the remaining quantities.
elements.sent-2411-151: Here $z=A^{1/2 e^{i\theta/2}$, where $A$ and $\theta$ are real.}
elements.sent-2411-152: The null tetrad transforms as \begin{equation}l'_\mu=Al_\mu,\quad m'_\mu=e^{i\theta}m_\mu,\quad n'_\mu=A^{-1}n_\mu,\end{equation} and we obtain, \begin{eqnarray}\rho' = A\rho, \mu' = A^{-1}\mu, \pi' = e^{-i\theta}\pi, \tau' = e^{ i\theta}\tau, \epsilon' = A(\epsilon-zDz^{-1}), \gamma' = A^{-1}(\gamma -z\Delta z^{-1}), \alpha' = e^{-i\theta}(\alpha -z\bar{\delta} z^{-1}), \beta' = e^{i\theta}(\beta -z\delta z^{-1}).\end{eqnarray}.
elements.formula-2411-080: \begin{equation}\rho' = A\rho, \mu' = A^{-1}\mu, \pi' = e^{-i\theta}\pi, \tau' = e^{ i\theta}\tau, \epsilon' = A(\epsilon-zDz^{-1}), \gamma' = A^{-1}(\gamma -z\Delta z^{-1}), \alpha' = e^{-i\theta}(\alpha -z\bar{\delta} z^{-1}), \beta' = e^{i\theta}(\beta -z\delta z^{-1}).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-153: We apply these transformations to the NUT metric in the following order:.
elements.sent-2411-154: For the NUT metric, the conditions $\tau=\pi=0$ are invariant.
elements.sent-2411-155: We have chosen to set $\epsilon'=0$ by defining $Dz$.
elements.sent-2411-156: Then further transformations should be compatible with $Dz=0$.
elements.sent-2411-157: We can set $\bar{\alpha '+\beta'=0$ as follows.. \begin{eqnarray}\bar{\alpha}'+\beta' = e^{i\theta}(\bar{\alpha}+\beta -\bar{z}\delta \bar{z}^{-1}-z\delta z^{-1}), \nonumber = e^{i\theta}\left(\bar{\alpha}+\beta +\frac{\delta \bar{z}}{\bar{z}} +\frac{\delta z}{z}\right), \nonumber = e^{i\theta}\left(\bar{\alpha}+\beta +\frac{\delta {A}}{A}\right).\end{eqnarray}.}
elements.sent-2411-158: Thus $\bar{\alpha '+\beta'$ can be set to zero, provided that the system $DA=0$ and $\delta A=-(\bar{\alpha +\beta)A$ is satisfied.}}
elements.sent-2411-159: Starting from the general expression of the commutator of $D$ and $\delta$ applied to $A$, we obtain \begin{eqnarray}(\delta D-D \delta)A = (\bar{\alpha}+\beta-\bar{\pi})DA +\kappa \Delta A -\sigma \bar{\delta} A -(\bar{\rho}+\epsilon-\bar{\epsilon})\delta A, \nonumber (\delta D-D \delta)A = (\bar{\alpha}+\beta)DA -\bar{\rho}\delta A,\nonumber -D \delta A = -\bar{\rho}\delta A.\end{eqnarray}.
elements.formula-2411-082: \begin{equation}(\delta D-D \delta)A = (\bar{\alpha}+\beta-\bar{\pi})DA +\kappa \Delta A -\sigma \bar{\delta} A -(\bar{\rho}+\epsilon-\bar{\epsilon})\delta A, \nonumber (\delta D-D \delta)A = (\bar{\alpha}+\beta)DA -\bar{\rho}\delta A,\nonumber -D \delta A = -\bar{\rho}\delta A.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-160: So, we can write the new form \begin{eqnarray}D (\bar{\alpha}+\beta) A = \bar{\rho}(\bar{\alpha}+\beta)A, \nonumber D (\bar{\alpha}+\beta) = \bar{\rho}(\bar{\alpha}+\beta),\nonumber \bar{\rho}\bar{\alpha}+\bar{\rho}\beta = \bar{\rho}(\bar{\alpha}+\beta).\end{eqnarray}.
elements.sent-2411-163: The imaginary part of $\gamma$ transforms as \begin{eqnarray}\gamma'-\bar{\gamma}' = A^{-1} \left( \gamma -\bar{\gamma} -z\Delta z^{-1}+\bar{z}\Delta \bar{z}^{-1} \right), \nonumber \gamma'-\bar{\gamma}' = A^{-1} \left( \gamma -\bar{\gamma} +\frac{\Delta z}{z}-\frac{\Delta \bar{z}}{\bar{z}} \right), \nonumber \gamma'-\bar{\gamma}' = A^{-1} \left( \gamma -\bar{\gamma} +i\Delta\theta\right).\end{eqnarray}.
elements.sent-2411-164: We look for the consistency of the equation \begin{eqnarray}A^{-1} \left( \gamma -\bar{\gamma} +i\Delta\theta\right) = \textstyle{\frac{1}{2}} \left( \frac{\Psi_2}{\rho'}-\frac{\bar{\Psi}_2}{\bar{\rho}'}\right), \nonumber A^{-1} \left( \gamma -\bar{\gamma} +i\Delta\theta\right) = \textstyle{\frac{1}{2}} \left( \frac{\Psi_2}{A\rho}-\frac{\bar{\Psi}_2}{A\bar{\rho}}\right), \nonumber \left( \gamma -\bar{\gamma} +i\Delta\theta\right) = \textstyle{\frac{1}{2}} \left( \frac{\Psi_2}{\rho}-\frac{\bar{\Psi}_2}{\bar{\rho}}\right).\end{eqnarray}.

Since $D	heta=0$, applying $D$ to the last equation we obtain,  egin{eqnarray}D\gamma -D ar{\gamma} +iD\Delta	heta = 	extstyle{ rac{1}{2}} \left(  rac{D\Psi_2}{ho}- rac{\Psi_2 Dho}{ho^2} - rac{D ar{\Psi}_2}{ ar{ho}} + rac{ ar{\Psi}_2 D ar{ho}}{ ar{ho}^2} ight),

onumber i D\Delta	heta = 0, 
onumber \Delta D	heta-(\gamma+ ar{\gamma})D	heta = 0,\end{eqnarray} and the last equation is identically satisfied since $D	heta=0$.
elements.sent-2411-166: Consequently, we can state that if we have \begin{equation}\kappa=\nu=\sigma=\lambda=\tau=\pi=0,\end{equation} then the $SL(2,C)$ transformations can be used to set \begin{equation}\epsilon=0,\quad \bar{\alpha}+\beta=0,\quad \gamma-\bar\gamma=\textstyle\frac{1}{2} \left(\frac{\Psi_2}{\rho}-\frac{\bar\Psi_2}{\bar\rho}\right)=0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-167: We will now show that, actually the NUT solution can be characterized solely by \begin{equation}\tau+\bar\pi=0,\end{equation} which is just the requirement that the distribution $\{D,\Delta\}$ is integrable.
elements.sent-2411-169: We first evaluate \begin{equation}\tau'-\bar\alpha'-\beta' = e^{i\theta}\left[ (\tau-\bar\alpha-\beta)+\bar z\bar\delta z^{-1}+ z\delta z^{-1} \right], \nonumber = e^{i\theta}\left[ (\tau-\bar\alpha-\beta)-\frac{\delta\bar z}{\bar z} -\frac{\delta z}{ z} \right], \nonumber = e^{i\theta}\left[ (\tau-\bar\alpha-\beta)-\frac{\delta A}{A} \right]=0.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-091: \begin{equation}\tau'-\bar\alpha'-\beta' = e^{i\theta}\left[ (\tau-\bar\alpha-\beta)+\bar z\bar\delta z^{-1}+ z\delta z^{-1} \right], \nonumber = e^{i\theta}\left[ (\tau-\bar\alpha-\beta)-\frac{\delta\bar z}{\bar z} -\frac{\delta z}{ z} \right], \nonumber = e^{i\theta}\left[ (\tau-\bar\alpha-\beta)-\frac{\delta A}{A} \right]=0.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-170:
Thus,  egin{equation}\delta A= (	au- ar\alpha- eta)A,\end{equation} hence, as $DA=0$, The commutator of $\Delta$ and $D$ applied to $A$ yields  egin{equation}\delta(DA) -D(\delta A)=- arho \delta A. 
onumber\end{equation} The left-hand side is  egin{equation}D\delta A =A(D	au-D ar\alpha-D eta), 
onumber\end{equation} and the right-hand side is given as  egin{equation} arho \delta A= A\left[ ho(	au+ ar\pi) - arho( ar\alpha+ ar\pi)-( arho eta) ight], 
onumber\end{equation} which finally yields  egin{equation}(	au+ ar\pi)(ho- arho)=0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-171: Thus we can set $\tau-\bar\alpha-\beta=0$ provided that either when $\tau+\bar\pi=0$ of $\rho=\bar\rho$.
elements.sent-2411-172: Case $\tau=0$ Assuming that $\tau+\bar\pi=0$ and $\tau-\bar\alpha-\beta=0$, we will show that $\tau=0$.
elements.sent-2411-173: For this, we need to check the integrability of the NP equations.
elements.sent-2411-174: Supposing \begin{equation}\kappa=\nu=\sigma=\lambda=0,\quad \epsilon=0,\quad \tau+\bar\pi=0,\quad \tau-\bar\alpha-\beta=0,\end{equation} we have the following equations for $\tau$: \begin{equation}D\tau=0,\quad \Delta\tau=(\gamma-\bar\gamma)\tau.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-178: Therefore if $\Psi_2-\bar\Psi_2$ is nonzero, then $\tau$ should be zero.
elements.sent-2411-180: Case $\Psi_2=\bar\Psi_2$ In this case, the Bianchi identities for $\Psi_2$ imply $\rho=\bar\rho$ and $\mu=\bar\mu$.
elements.sent-2411-181: The relevant NP equations give \begin{equation}D\tau=0,\quad D\alpha=\rho(\alpha+\pi),\quad D\beta=\bar\rho\beta,\quad -\bar\delta\pi=\pi^2 +(\alpha-\bar\beta)\pi.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-183:
The commutator of $D$ and $ ar\delta$ applied to $\pi$ gives  egin{equation}D ar\delta\pi =ho ar\delta\pi, 
onumber D(-\pi^2 -(\alpha- ar eta)\pi) =ho(-\pi^2 -(\alpha- ar eta)\pi), 
onumber -\pi D(\alpha- ar eta) =ho(-\pi^2 -(\alpha- ar eta)\pi), 
onumber -\piho(\alpha+\pi)+\piho ar eta =\piho(-\pi -(\alpha- ar eta)), 
onumber 2\piho ar eta =0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-184: Thus if $\beta$ is nonzero, then $\pi=0$, hence $\tau=\bar\alpha+\beta=0$.
elements.sent-2411-185: Case $\beta=0$ In this case, the relevant NP equations are \begin{equation}\bar\delta\alpha=0,\quad \delta\alpha=\mu\rho+\alpha\bar\alpha-\Psi_2, \quad \delta\rho=\rho(\bar\alpha+\beta), \quad \bar\delta\mu=-\mu(\alpha+\bar\beta).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-188: Proposition We require to preserve the condition, \begin{equation}\left(\gamma' + \overline{\gamma}'\right)\left(\rho' + \overline{\rho}'\right) + \mu'\rho' - \overline{\mu}' \overline{\rho}' - \left(\Psi_2' + \overline{\Psi}_2'\right)=0,\end{equation} as $\rho'=A \rho$ and $\mu'=\frac{1 {A \mu$, we have \begin{equation}A\left(\gamma' + \overline{\gamma}'\right) \left( \rho - \overline{\rho}\right) + \mu \rho-\overline{\mu} \overline{\rho} +\left(\Psi_2' + \overline{\Psi}_2'\right) = 0, \nonumber A \left(\gamma' + \overline{\gamma}'\right)\left( \rho - \overline{\rho}\right) - \left(\gamma + \overline{\gamma}\right)\left(\rho-\overline{\rho}\right) = 0, \nonumber \left(\rho-\overline{\rho}\right) \left[A \left(\gamma' + \overline{\gamma}'\right)-\left(\gamma + \overline{\gamma}\right)\right] = 0.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-189: So if $\rho=\overline{\rho}$ we deduce that this condition is satisfied.
elements.sent-2411-190: Case $\rho \neq \overline{\rho$ Finally, in this case $\gamma' + \overline{\gamma ' = \frac{1 {A \left(\gamma + \overline{\gamma}\right)$ must be maintained.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-191: We calculate that \begin{equation}\frac{1}{A}\left[\left(\gamma + \frac{1}{2} \frac{\Delta A}{A} + \frac{i}{2} \Delta \theta\right) + \left(\overline{\gamma} + \frac{1}{2} \frac{\Delta A}{A} - \frac{i}{2} \Delta \theta\right)\right] = \frac{1}{A} \left(\gamma + \overline{\gamma}\right),\end{equation} thus the last equation is satisfied since $\Delta A=0$.. \bibliographystyle{apsrev4-2.}
